course_description:
This foundation course provides students with an introduction to the profession of speech pathology. Topics included relate to ethical practice, professional conduct, self-reflection and lifelong learning and communication. Students are introduced to person and family centredness, the International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health, the principles of cultural and linguistic responsiveness and inter-professionalism and participate in professional skill development through a simulated patient clinic.

Welcome to SPCH7131 and the Master of Speech Pathology Studies program! In SPCH7131, students will begin their journey towards becoming practicing speech pathologists. This foundation-level course provides students with an introduction to the profession of speech pathology. This course is presented in three modules: (1) foundations of speech pathology practice; (2) communication and swallowing: from paediatric development to adult disorders; and (3) from theory to therapy: practical applications in communication and swallowing disorders. Learning activities will explore the professional role, conduct, and communication skills of a speech pathologist, with a focus on self-reflection and lifelong learning, cultural and linguistic responsiveness, person- and family-centred care, and interprofessional practice. Students will also explore the typical and disordered communication and swallowing skills of children and adults across the lifespan.

In this course, students will independently engage with online learning resources while having authentic learning experiences with peers and teaching staff in weekly tutorials which will involve participation in all learning activities. Students will also engage in a program of simulation learning experiences across the lifespan to provide an opportunity to apply theory into practice.

Additional timetable information
A detailed course outline for SPCH7131 is provided on the SPCH7131 Blackboard site. Please note that students are expected to attend all tutorials and simulation program sessions. Due to the nature of the learning activities, these tutorials and practical sessions are not recorded.ﾠ
course_aims:
In this course students will begin their journey to becoming a practising speech pathologist and is divided into three modules: (1) foundations of speech pathology practice, (2) communication and swallowing: from paediatric development to adult disorders; and (3) from theory to therapy: practical applications in communication and swallowing disorders.

This foundation level course provides students with an introduction to the speech pathology profession.

The course focuses on the professional role, conduct and communication skills of speech pathologists, with a focus on self-reflection and lifelong learning, cultural and linguistic responsiveness, person- and family-centred care, and interprofessional practice. In this course students are introduced to Speech Pathology Australia's Professional Standards for Speech Pathologists in Australia (2020) and Speech Pathology Australia's Code of Ethics (2020), documents that guide speech pathologists in their professional role.

In this course, students will independently engage with online learning resources while having authentic learning experiences with peers and teaching staff in weekly tutorials which will involve participation in all learning activities. Students will also engage in a program of simulation learning experiences across the lifespan to provide an opportunity to apply theory into practice.

learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate foundation knowledge relating to the standards of professional speech pathology practice, ethics, reflective practice and life-long learning (SPA PS 1.1, 1.2, 1.3, 2.1, 2.2, 2.3, 2.5, 2.6 and 3.1)

Describe and apply the principles of person- and family-centred care, evidence-based practice, inter-professional practice and the International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (2001) to speech pathology practice (SPA PS 1.1, 1.4, 1.6, 2.2, 2.5, 2.6 and 3.1)
Explain the principles of culturally responsive practice with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples and communities (SPA PS 1.1, 1.4, 1.5, 1.6, 2.1, 2.2, 2.5, 2.6 and 3.1)
Explain the principles of cultural and linguistic responsiveness as relating to speech pathology practice (SPA PS 1.1, 1.4, 1.5, 1.6, 2.1, 2.2, 2.5, 2.6 and 3.1)
Describe the components of typical and disordered communication and swallowing in populations across the lifespan (SPA PS 1.1, 1.3, 1.4, 1.5, 1.6, 1.7, 2.5, 2.6, 3.1, 3.2, 3.3)
Use effective verbal and non-verbal communication and critical reflection skills across a variety of contexts in collaboration with peers (SPA PS 1.1, 1.5, 2.1, 2.2 and 2.6)

assessment_details.title: Reflective Practice Portfolio
assessment_details.due_date: Reflection 1 (Formative) 5/12/2025 8:00 am Reflection 2 12/12/2025 8:00 am Reflection 3 23/12/2025 8:00 am Reflection 4 9/01/2026 8:00 am Reflection 5 30/01/2026 8:00 am
assessment_details.task_description: This assessment focuses on the development of your reflective practice and lifelong learning skills, which underpins excellence in speech pathology practice. Students are required to submit a Professional Practice Reflection Portfolio consisting of five (5) structured audio-visual recorded reflective tasks using the 5R Reflective Framework to facilitate the development of these skills. These reflections will be informed by the topics and learning objectives addressed within the SPCH7131 online learning activities and tutorials. Each reflection is due at 8am after the relevant learning activity. The first reflection is a formative reflection which will be marked and you will receive feedback. Each subsequent reflection is worth 5% (total 20%), with a limit of 2-3 minutes. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submit via ePortfolio (links in assessment folder on SPCH7131 Blackboard site).
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Communication and Swallowing Disorders Group Presentation
assessment_details.due_date: 23/01/2026 8:00 am One member of the group is to submit PowerPoint slides to Blackboard assignments by 8am Friday 23 rd January.
assessment_details.task_description: This assessment allows students to demonstrate their ability to observe, interpret, and describe communication and swallowing in applied contexts. Each group will be allocated a short video recording depicting a client who presents with a communication or swallowing disorder. Your group will: Introduce the video - identify the client’s age, context, and purpose of the interaction. Discuss the aetiology of the communication or swallowing disorder. Describe the observable communication or swallowing behaviours evident in the video: (1) identify features of typical functioning for the person’s lifespan stage and (2) identify any characteristics consistent with disorder or difference. Compare typical and disordered features, explaining possible contributing factors (developmental, neurological, anatomical, environmental). Apply the ICF framework to the case, outlining: (1) body functions and structures involved, (2) activities and participation impacts and (3) environmental and personal factors influencing communication or swallowing Describe the role of the speech pathologist in supporting this individual, including potential assessment and management considerations. Demonstrate teamwork and clear communication throughout the presentation. Each group will have 10 minutes to present followed by 2–3 minutes for questions . All group members must contribute equitably to the preparation and presentation. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct When a student is required to complete an oral or practical assessment item worth 20% or more, the performance will be recorded and retained per Appendix 1 ( Assessment Procedure (95). assessment_details.title: Cultural Safety Module
assessment_details.task_description: Students are required to complete the online Cultural Safety Modules 1 and 2 by Tuesday 27th January and complete the short answer quiz for this on the SPCH7131 Blackboard site. Students will be automatically enrolled in the UQ Extend course for the HMBS Faculty with a link available on the SPCH7131 Blackboard site. Students must complete the online quiz via the SPCH7131 Blackboard site. This quiz will have 2 short answer questions and students will be marked on whether responses to the questions are appropriate, evidenced by module content and resources. Inappropriate or derogatory/disrespectful answers will fail this assessment. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.topic: Online pre-recorded lectures In week 1 and 4, there are three online pre-recorded lectures designed for independent, self-paced learning. Each lecture takes approximately 2 hours to complete and provides and introduction to the course and foundational paediatric development knowledge.

Weekly On-Campus Tutorials Tutorials will be held in person on campus during Weeks 1–3 and 5-8 of the semester. Each session will run for 2 hours and will incorporate small group discussions to explore key concepts in greater depth. These sessions are designed as experiential learning activities, and participants are expected to attend and actively engage. Occasionally, to prepare, students must complete pre-tutorial learning activities. As these sessions involve small group work, they will not be recorded.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 5 To Week 8 (05 Jan - 01 Feb)
learning_activities.topic: In-Person Simulation Program Students will participate in 4 x 4hour (9am-1pm) simulation-based learning workshops across both paediatric and adult areas of practice. In the paediatric module, students will develop their skills in information gathering and in the adult module, students will develop their skills in the assessment of communication and swallowing. Students will have the opportunity to interact with a variety of simulated patients (SPs) and Practice Educators (PEs) in order to develop their clinical reasoning and professional skills. Participation in these practical sessions is compulsory.
